text: Hello, I'm sharing an extended right colectomy. This is a patient who on an original colonoscopy was found to have four separate right-sided colon cancers. They were all very small polyps, between one and two centimeters, but all positive for well-differentiated adeno. all completely removed with negative margins and the most distal polyp was in the proximal transverse colon and a tattoo was placed distal to that there were no distal polyps and after genetic testing and consultation the patient opted for a colectomy as definitive management. So just going ahead, I took the ileocolic pedicle there. This is an elderly individual. I did not necessarily think they would benefit from a CME as well as the fact that the tumors were all well differentiated, very small and completely resected with endoscopy. We're working our way over the head of the pancreas towards the right colic vessels. So some of the times when I do extended rights, I do a more transverse orientation of the ports. I did my standard left upper to suprapubic port position for this case and it worked out for me. You'll see in the middle of the case, I will actually port hop the camera from three to two, which actually helps with the dissection over the transverse colon. So that's that tattoo that was placed distal to the most distal cancer and a marking out where I want my mesenteric transection to be from the middle colic up to that margin. So these are the right colic vessels here.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to take the middle colic vein and artery, demonstrating the transection line through the mesentery from the planned distal margin.

The surgeon splits the omentum over the planned margin of the transverse colon, preparing to resect it with the specimen. The camera has been switched from port three to two, allowing for better visualization of the small bowel mesentery and inferior dissection. The surgeon works with two right and one left instrument, joining the medially dissected plane.

The surgeon dissects through the gastrocolic omentum into the lesser sac, navigating around the lateral attachments near the hepatic flexure. The sponge marks the area of previous medial dissection. The right colon is fully mobilized, and the surgeon uses a stapler at the distal transverse colon and then staples the small bowel, with the camera positioned in arm two.
The surgeon positions the stapler at the stapled end of the colon to create an isoparastaltic anastomosis, ensuring the stapler fits properly from this angle. The anastomosis is then sewn closed.

The final pathology proved that there was actually no residual carcinoma in the right colon. There were a couple of other small adenomas in the right colon and the margins were clean. And the patient did very well and happy with their decision. And I hope you enjoyed.
